1: A revelation of God: "Afterward Jesus appeared again to his disciples, by the Sea of Galilee." The third resurrection appearance (v14) moves from Jerusalem to Galilee — the region of the ministry's beginning, the place of calling and commissioning. The appearance by the sea echoes the original calling of the disciples in the Synoptics (Matthew 4:18–22; Mark 1:16–20), bringing the Gospel's narrative arc full circle: from the calling of disciples by the sea, through the ministry, the passion, and the resurrection, back to the sea. The Evangelist's epilogue is a commission that returns to the site of the calling so that the disciples understand their mission as the continuation and extension of the ministry they witnessed.
2: A revelation of God: the seven disciples present — Simon Peter, Thomas, Nathanael, the sons of Zebedee, and two unnamed others — include the doubter who confessed (Thomas, v28), the one who will be three-times denied and restored (Peter), and the one from Cana where the first sign was performed (Nathanael, John 2:1–11; John 1:45–51). The Evangelist assembles the epilogue's cast from the Gospel's significant figures, closing the narrative circle that began with the calling of Nathanael and the wedding at Cana.
3: A revelation of God: "but that night they caught nothing." The disciples' return to fishing — their pre-call occupation — without Christ's direction produces nothing. The night catch is the figure of fruitlessness apart from union with Christ (John 15:5 — "apart from me you can do nothing"). The Evangelist uses the empty nets to create the context in which the risen Christ's direction transforms futility into abundance. The disciples are not condemned for going fishing; they are given the opportunity to experience again, in the resurrection context, what they experienced at the first calling: their fishing is inadequate; the Lord's direction is not.
4: A revelation of God: "Early in the morning, Jesus stood on the shore, but the disciples did not realize that it was Jesus." The morning non-recognition parallels Mary's non-recognition in the garden (John 20:14). The risen Christ is present on the shore before the disciples are aware of him; he comes to them in their frustration and exhaustion. The Evangelist's pattern is consistent: the risen Christ appears to disciples who are not looking for him — Mary weeping at the tomb, disciples behind locked doors, fishermen returning empty. The resurrection appearances are initiated by the risen Christ, not produced by the disciples' expectation.
5: A revelation of God: "Friends, haven't you any fish?" The risen Christ's question — "do you have any food to eat?" (prosphagion, literally something eaten with bread, particularly fish) — is the opening of the engagement. The answer "No" is the honest acknowledgment of the empty nets that will be the occasion for the miraculous catch. The Evangelist's address paidia (children / lads) is affectionate — the same term used in 1 John 2:14, 18. The risen Christ engages his disciples in their ordinary failure with gentle interrogation that is the beginning of provision.
6: A direct type: "Throw your net on the right side of the boat and you will find some." The miraculous catch of fish that follows the risen Christ's direction deliberately echoes the call narrative in Luke 5:4–7, where the first miraculous catch of fish follows Jesus' instruction and Peter's humble response. The Evangelist's epilogue creates a conscious parallel: at the beginning of the ministry, a miraculous catch of fish followed by a call to discipleship; at the end of the resurrection appearances, a miraculous catch of fish followed by a threefold commission (vv15–17). The parallel frames the entirety of the disciples' call as the risen Christ's work from beginning to end.
7: A revelation of God: "the disciple whom Jesus loved said to Peter, 'It is the Lord!'" The Beloved Disciple again recognizes first (cf. John 20:8 — believed at the empty tomb); Peter again acts first (cf. John 20:6 — entered the tomb). The complementary pair functions together: the contemplative recognition of the Beloved Disciple and the impulsive action of Peter. That Peter wraps his outer garment around him before jumping into the water is an act of reverence: you do not approach the Lord naked (cf. Genesis 3:21 — the need for covering in the presence of God). He clothes himself to meet the risen Christ on the shore.
8: A revelation of God: the other disciples follow in the boat, towing the full net. They cannot leave the net — the abundance of the catch makes it impossible to abandon. The net full of fish that cannot be hauled in (v6) is the harvest of the mission that the disciples cannot accomplish by themselves but drag toward shore by the risen Christ's direction. The community of disciples together — some in the water, some in the boat — bring the catch to the shore where the Lord awaits. The church's mission is accomplished not by individual heroism but by the community moving together toward the Christ who is already at the destination.
9: A direct revelation: "they saw a fire of burning coals there with fish on it, and some bread." Anthrakia (charcoal fire) appears in only two places in the entire NT: John 18:18, where Peter warms himself by a charcoal fire in the high priest's courtyard and denies Jesus three times; and here, John 21:9, where the risen Christ has prepared a charcoal fire for breakfast. The Evangelist's precise choice of vocabulary is the signal that the breakfast by the sea is the scene of Peter's restoration. The same kind of fire at which Peter denied becomes the fire at which he will be restored. The risen Christ has prepared the scene — fire, fish, bread — for the triple commission that will answer the triple denial.
10: A revelation of God: "Bring some of the fish you have just caught." The risen Christ invites the disciples to contribute to the meal from the catch that his direction has made possible. The pattern is Eucharistic: the risen Christ who provides — as at the feeding of the five thousand (John 6:11 — "Jesus then took the loaves, gave thanks, and distributed to those who were seated") — also incorporates the disciples' labor into the provision. The five loaves and two fish that the disciples brought to Jesus for the feeding of the multitude are echoed here: the disciples' catch joins the fish the Lord has already prepared.
11: A revelation of God: "So Simon Peter climbed back into the boat and dragged the net ashore. It was full of large fish, 153, but even with so many the net was not torn." The 153 fish has generated extensive allegorical speculation (Jerome: 153 species of fish = the totality of nations; Augustine: mathematical symbolism). The Evangelist offers no interpretive key and the Evangelist rarely includes numerically precise details without purpose (John 6:19 — twenty-five or thirty stadia; John 11:39 — "four days"). Whatever the number signifies, two details are certain: the catch is enormous, and the net is not torn. In John 10:16, Jesus spoke of "one flock"; in John 15:1–8, he spoke of the branches remaining in the vine producing fruit. The net that holds the enormous catch without tearing is the community of disciples that the mission will produce — held together by the one who is the vine, the shepherd, the risen Lord who directs the nets.
12: A revelation of God: "Come and have breakfast." The risen Christ's invitation to the meal is an act of extraordinary intimacy — the sharing of food as the restoration of fellowship. The disciples "knew it was the Lord" without needing to be told, yet "none of the disciples dared ask him, 'Who are you?'" The reverential certainty is the post-resurrection experience of knowing the risen Christ: he is familiar and yet different, recognizable and yet beyond ordinary familiarity. The breakfast on the shore is the enacted form of the promise in Revelation 3:20: "I will come in and eat with that person, and they with me."
13: A shadow: "Jesus came, took the bread and gave it to them, and did the same with the fish." The action — taking bread, giving it to them — echoes the feeding of the five thousand (John 6:11: "Jesus then took the loaves, gave thanks, and distributed to those who were seated") and the Last Supper tradition preserved in the Synoptics. The Evangelist presents the risen Christ's breakfast distribution as the continuation of the same pattern: the one who fed the multitude with five loaves feeds his disciples with bread and fish on the resurrection morning. The Eucharistic resonance is present without being forced: every meal the risen Christ shares with his disciples carries the weight of the one meal that gathered all the significance of his life and death.
14: A revelation of God: "This was now the third time Jesus appeared to his disciples after he was raised from the dead." The Evangelist's counting — third appearance — marks the completeness of the resurrection witness. Three witnesses establish a matter (Deuteronomy 19:15); three appearances to the gathered disciples establish the resurrection as a reality among them. The third appearance, like the third day of the resurrection and the threefold restoration of Peter that follows, carries the number of completion and fullness. The resurrection has been fully demonstrated; the disciples are fully equipped; the commission will be complete.
15: A direct revelation: "Simon son of John, do you love me more than these?" The triple question — "do you love me?" asked three times — directly and deliberately reverses the triple denial at the charcoal fire of John 18:17–27. The Evangelist's structural symmetry is precise: three denials at a charcoal fire / three affirmations at a charcoal fire. The name "Simon son of John" — the pre-call name before Jesus renamed him Peter (John 1:42) — recalls the man before the call, the man who will now be re-commissioned. The question uses agapaō (love as moral commitment and devotion); Peter responds with phileō (love as personal affection). Whether the Evangelist intends a sharp distinction or is using the terms as near-synonyms is disputed; at minimum, Peter does not match the level of the question but offers what he genuinely has.
16: A revelation of God: "Again Jesus said, 'Simon son of John, do you love me?'" The second question — the second of the three that will undo the three denials — is asked without the comparative ("more than these"). Jesus removes the implicit comparison with the other disciples; the question now is simply about Peter's own love for the Son. The commission this time is to "take care of my sheep" — the shepherding language of chapter 10 (the Good Shepherd) is given to Peter as the task that flows from love for the shepherd. Peter is not replacing the Good Shepherd; he is exercising under-shepherding derived from and accountable to the one who lays down his life for the sheep.
17: A direct revelation: "The third time he said to him, 'Simon son of John, do you love me?' Peter was hurt because Jesus asked him the third time." The third question uses phileō — the word Peter has been using — as if now meeting Peter at his own level. Peter's hurt is the recognition that the three questions have been as deliberate as the three denials. The restoration is being performed with surgical precision: each denial is being addressed, each "I don't know the man" is being answered with "you know that I love you." The final commission — "Feed my sheep" — restores Peter to the apostolic vocation that his denial had seemingly forfeited. The grace of the risen Christ is not the grace that overlooks failure but the grace that restores through a deliberate repetition of the failure's form.
18: A direct revelation: "when you are old you will stretch out your hands, and someone else will dress you and lead you where you do not want to go." The prophecy of Peter's martyrdom — interpreted explicitly in v19 — uses the language of crucifixion: "stretch out your hands" is the posture of one being bound to a cross. The early church understood Peter's death by crucifixion (Clement of Rome, Tertullian) as the fulfillment of this word. Jesus' prophecy to Peter at the restoration is simultaneously the prediction of his martyrdom — the most extreme form of the discipleship to which "follow me" calls. The one who denied to avoid death is called to follow into death. The denial at the charcoal fire becomes the cross that Peter will embrace.
19: A direct revelation: "Jesus said this to indicate the kind of death by which Peter would glorify God. Then he said to him, 'Follow me!'" The Evangelist's editorial comment — "to indicate the kind of death by which Peter would glorify God" — is the Christological interpretation of martyrdom: the disciple's death glorifies God in the same way the Son's death glorified God (John 12:23, 28; John 13:31). The martyr's death is the ultimate form of following the shepherd who lays down his life (John 10:11, 15), the seed that falls to the ground and dies (John 12:24), and the one who loved his own to the end (John 13:1). "Follow me" at the end of the Gospel is the same call as at the beginning (John 1:43 — "Follow me"); now Peter knows what following means and accepts it.
20: A revelation of God: "Peter turned and saw that the disciple whom Jesus loved was following them." The Beloved Disciple is the witness to Peter's restoration just as he was the witness to the crucifixion (John 19:26–27) and the empty tomb (John 20:8). His presence in the epilogue is the Evangelist's way of maintaining the testimony-chain: Peter's restoration and commission are witnessed by the same disciple whose testimony grounds the Gospel. The Beloved Disciple's following is both physical (walking behind them) and the deeper following that the Gospel commends: the discipleship of witness and contemplation that will produce the Gospel itself (v24).
21: A revelation of God: "Lord, what about him?" Peter's question — immediately after receiving the commission and the prophecy of his martyrdom — is the instinctive comparison of one's own calling with another's. The Evangelist presents this as the natural but misdirected response to a personal call: instead of receiving it fully, Peter looks at the Beloved Disciple and wonders if he will have a different fate. The question is answered with the rebuke that serves all subsequent disciples who compare their vocations with others': "what is that to you? You must follow me."
22: A revelation of God: "If I want him to remain alive until I return, what is that to you? You must follow me." The risen Christ's sovereignty over the vocations and destinies of his disciples — the martyr and the witness, Peter and the Beloved Disciple — is the final word on discipleship in the Gospel. Peter will die for the Lord; the Beloved Disciple will live to write the testimony. Both are acts of following; neither is superior to the other; both are the Lord's to determine. The last word the risen Christ speaks to Peter about discipleship is "follow me" — the same word he spoke at the first call (John 1:43). The Gospel ends where it began: with the call to follow the Son.
23: A revelation of God: "the rumor spread among the believers that this disciple would not die." The community's misunderstanding of Jesus' conditional statement — "if I want him to remain until I return" is not a promise that he would remain — is the Evangelist's final reminder of how easily the community constructs its own expectations around the words of Jesus rather than receiving them as they are. The Evangelist corrects the misunderstanding directly, modeling the hermeneutical discipline the Gospel itself has been practicing: do not read more into the words of Jesus than the words themselves carry. The correction of this misunderstanding is itself an act of faithful witness.
24: A direct revelation: "This is the disciple who testifies to these things and who wrote them down. We know that his testimony is true." The Beloved Disciple is identified as the author of the Gospel's testimony — the eyewitness at the cross (John 19:35), the one who first believed at the empty tomb (John 20:8), the one who recognized the risen Christ on the shore (John 21:7). The "we know" of the final verse is the community's endorsement of the testimony — the elders of the Johannine community confirming the witness that has been preserved. The Gospel rests on the eyewitness testimony of one who was present at the critical moments of the passion, resurrection, and restoration. The Beloved Disciple's testimony is not secondhand; it is the foundation of the community's faith.
25: A revelation of God: "Jesus did many other things as well. If every one of them were written down, I suppose that even the whole world would not have room for the books that would be written." The closing hyperbole is the Evangelist's final testimony to the inexhaustibility of the incarnate Son. The Gospel has selected from an infinite supply; what has been written is sufficient for faith (John 20:31); what has not been written is beyond the capacity of the world to contain. The figure is not mere rhetorical flourish — it is the Evangelist's statement about who Christ is: the one whose acts and words overflow every container, whose life cannot be exhausted by any text, any canon, any world. John 1:1 began "in the beginning was the Word"; John 21:25 ends by acknowledging that the Word's story cannot be ended. The Gospel that could not contain the Logos stops not because the story is over but because the story is inexhaustible.
